tagline: Canada's busiest World Cup venue, where the home side fights for a knockout spot under a downtown retractable roof.

why.title: Canada's Home Base
why.text: Both of Canada's decisive group games are here, vs Qatar and vs Switzerland. This is where the host nation lives in the group stage.

why.title: Busiest in Canada
why.text: Seven matches, more than any other Canadian venue, and the only one going beyond the Round of 32 with two knockout games.

why.title: The Retractable Roof
why.text: Billed as the world's largest cable-supported retractable roof, it tucks into a central pod above the videoboard and opens or closes in about 20 minutes.

why.title: Grass Grown for FIFA
why.text: Crews ripped out the artificial turf and laid a hybrid natural pitch in spring 2026, grown in the Fraser Valley and kept alive with six grow-light rigs.

gettingThere: BC Place sits right in downtown Vancouver, so most of the core is a 10-20 minute walk away. From YVR airport, ride the Canada Line SkyTrain to Waterfront, then one Expo Line stop to Stadium-Chinatown, about 30-35 minutes total for roughly CA$10.
transit: Expo Line to Stadium-Chinatown station is the closest, a 5-minute walk; exit to Beatty Street for the main gates.
Alternate route: Canada Line to Yaletown-Roundhouse, then a 10-minute walk northeast through Yaletown.
Driving and parking are strongly discouraged on match days; use a regional Park & Ride and finish by SkyTrain.
Tap a Compass Card or contactless to skip ticket lines; budget about CA$10 round-trip from the airport.
matchday: The stadium hugs the north shore of False Creek beside Yaletown, steps from Rogers Arena and the adjacent Parq Vancouver casino-resort. The official FIFA Fan Festival runs free at Hastings Park (PNE grounds) from June 11 to July 19, showing 70-plus matches on big screens.
food: Yaletown next door for craft breweries, cocktail bars, and patios within a short walk.
Gastown's cobblestone streets and lively bars, plus nearby Chinatown for late eats.
Parq Vancouver beside the stadium has a 50-foot sportsbook screen and restaurants like The Victor.
Vancouver runs strong on fresh seafood, sushi, and Asian cuisine across downtown.
tips: Get there by SkyTrain, not car; Stadium-Chinatown is a 5-minute walk and parking gridlocks after the whistle.
Beat the post-match crush by walking 10-15 minutes to Yaletown-Roundhouse, a far less mobbed station.
Pack a light jacket for evening kickoffs, which cool to 11-13C, and sunscreen for day games and the Fan Festival.
The roof handles any rain, so never let a forecast deter you from attending.
Lower bowl behind the goals carries the loudest Whitecaps Southsiders energy; west-side mid-tier seats face the videoboard and Northern Lights facade.
Clear-bag policy is in effect: bags must be clear plastic, max 12 by 6 by 12 inches.
weather: June and July in Vancouver are mild and dry with highs around 18-21C, long daylight, and a UV index up to 8; pack sunscreen and a light layer for cool evenings.
lore: Opened in 1983 for Expo 86 as the world's largest air-supported domed stadium, before a 2011 overhaul swapped the inflatable roof for the retractable one.
Hosted the 2015 FIFA Women's World Cup Final, where the USA beat Japan 5-2 on a Carli Lloyd hat-trick.
Staged the 2010 Winter Olympics opening and closing ceremonies and has hosted the CFL's Grey Cup 10 times.
Its all-time attendance record is 65,061 for an Ed Sheeran concert in September 2023; the venue's LED Northern Lights facade animates the night skyline.

transport.summary: BC Place is in the heart of downtown Vancouver, so transit and walking dominate; driving is strongly discouraged. Critical World Cup change: the usual Stadium-Chinatown exit beside the stadium is closed on match days, so all ticketed fans ride to Main Street-Science World station and walk the car-free Last Mile promenade to the only spectator entrance at Carrall Street and Pacific Boulevard. Pacific Boulevard is closed to traffic from May 23 through end of July.

rail.name: Expo Line (SkyTrain) - match-day route
rail.detail: On all seven match days fans must use this station, not Stadium-Chinatown, whose adjacent Expo Boulevard exit is closed. Exit and join the Last Mile car-free walk along Quebec Street and Pacific Boulevard to the Carrall Street entrance. The spectator route opens 4 hours before kickoff; aim to start walking at least 1 hour pre-match.
rail.deal: Trains run through downtown every 2 to 2.5 minutes before and after matches; service extended one hour later on June 13, June 26, and July 2.

rail.name: Expo Line (SkyTrain) - non-match-day
rail.detail: Closest station for non-match events: up the stairs to Beatty Street, turn left, about a 5-minute walk to Gates A, B and H. Beatty and Keefer Street entrances stay open year-round; only the lower Expo Boulevard exit closes on World Cup match days.

points.t: turn left to Gates A, B, H
only Expo Boulevard exit closes match days

rail.name: Canada Line (SkyTrain)
rail.detail: Direct line from YVR airport; on the downtown peninsula side, walk northeast along Mainland Street then Robson, roughly 10 minutes, to reach the Last Mile secondary entrance from Keefer Street. Fare gate capacity here was boosted 66% for the crowds.

bus.name: Downtown service boost
bus.detail: TransLink adds about 600 extra bus trips per day during the tournament, with more frequency on downtown routes including R5, 14, 19 and 23. SeaBus runs every 10 minutes before and after matches for North Shore fans.

bus.name: Fan Festival shuttle
bus.from: Expo and Millennium Lines to PNE
bus.detail: Dedicated shuttle connects the FIFA Fan Festival at Hastings Park/PNE to both SkyTrain lines, plus extra service on existing PNE routes.

shuttle.name: West Coast Express specials
shuttle.detail: Special round-trip commuter-rail service on Saturday June 13 and Sunday June 21 only: a westbound train leaves Mission about 4 hours before kickoff and an eastbound returns from Waterfront about 2 hours after the final whistle.

transport.fromAirport: From YVR, ride the Canada Line SkyTrain straight downtown (about 25 minutes, no transfer), then transfer at Waterfront to the Expo Line. On match days continue to Main Street-Science World rather than Stadium-Chinatown. Total roughly 30-40 minutes; budget about CA$10 including the airport surcharge added on departures from YVR.
transport.rideshare: Do not set Uber, Lyft or taxi to the stadium door on match days; road closures and the secure footprint make it impossible. The official drop-off is 1510 Quebec Street (Quebec and Terminal, beside Science World), with a secondary point at 525 W 2nd Avenue near Olympic Village station; walk the Last Mile from there. Tipping rideshare/taxi 15-20% is customary in Canada.
transport.bike: Free supervised Bike Valet takes bicycles, e-bikes and scooters near the stadium, and Mobi public bikeshare docks sit throughout the area. The scenic False Creek Seawall is a flat, car-free ride from much of the city.
tips: TransLink is not surging fares for the World Cup; pay with a Compass card, contactless credit/debit, or a mobile wallet by tapping in and out.
Memorize the swap: ride to Main Street-Science World, not Stadium-Chinatown, on every match day.
Post-match trains are packed; walk 10-15 minutes into Yaletown or Gastown and board a quieter station, or grab food first to let the first wave clear.
A DayPass (about CA$12.55) beats single fares if you are riding several times on match day.

parking.summary: Downtown parking is very limited and strongly discouraged on match days, when Pacific Boulevard and surrounding streets are closed to general traffic. Treat any drive as park-on-the-edge-and-ride: stash the car at a regional TransLink Park and Ride and finish on SkyTrain. Lots nearest the stadium normally run CA$10 to CA$30 and fill fast.

lots.name: Stadium-area event lots (Rogers Arena / Plaza of Nations vicinity)
lots.detail: The cluster of lots that serve Rogers Arena and BC Place events; expect higher event pricing, early sellouts, and slow exits, with match-day road closures cutting access right around the stadium.

points.t: serve Rogers Arena and BC Place

parking.prepaid: If you must drive, reserve ahead through SpotHero or similar so you have a guaranteed spot and a fixed price; do not count on day-of street parking near the venue on a match day.
parking.accessible: A dedicated accessible drop-off (the 147 Accessible Entrance) is at street level on Pacific Boulevard under Gate F for guests using mobility devices; on-site accessible parking is very limited. HandyDART door-to-door service is available to eligible visitors by application.
parking.tailgating: No stadium tailgating; this is a dense downtown venue with no surface tailgate lots. Pre-match atmosphere lives in Yaletown and Gastown pubs and at the FIFA Fan Festival at Hastings Park.
tips: Skip driving entirely; SkyTrain plus the Last Mile walk is faster than fighting closures and CA$30 lots.
If a car is unavoidable, use a regional Park and Ride and ride the Expo Line into Main Street-Science World.
Accessible drop-off is under Gate F on Pacific Boulevard; arrive early as match-day closures reroute approaches.
